title: Whittled hazel walking stick
subtitle: A coppice-hazel walking stick cut from a straight-grown pole and finished with a sloyd knife
excerpt: A hazel walking stick comes from the same material as the classic hurdle stake: a straight-grown coppice pole, the bark cleaned back, the handle carved to fit the hand, and the tip shod or left as a taper. This intermediate project covers pole selection, bark removal, handle carving, and a boiled-linseed finish that protects against rain.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for stick selection and handle carving in hazel and ash. Beeler, The Tools That Built America (1976) for the American walking-staff tradition in equivalent species.

content.text: Hazel coppice produces more usable walking-stick
blanks

per acre than almost any other British woodland. The poles grow straight and even from the stool, reach handle height in four to seven years, and season to a dense, smooth-barked shaft in six to twelve months. A well-selected and
seasoned
hazel pole needs very little carving work to become a serviceable walking stick; the effort is in the selection and the drying, not the knife.
What you need: wood

A hazel coppice pole, cut in winter when the sap is low. Length: handle height plus 15 cm (typically 105 to 115 cm for an adult stick). Diameter: 22 to 28 mm at the grip, tapering to 18 to 20 mm at the tip. The pole should be straight for at least the bottom 60 cm; some natural curve at the handle end is acceptable and often desirable for grip comfort. Dry for six months to a year, upright or hanging, before shaping the handle. A stick worked too green will lose its bark cleanly during drying but will also shrink and the handle carving will loosen from the grip shape.
What you need: tools

A sloyd knife for handle shaping. A straight drawknife to clean bark from the shaft if the bark has not naturally loosened during drying. A leather strop. Sandpaper at 120 and 180 grit. Boiled linseed oil.
Safety

Four-finger grip on the work; every push cut travels away from the body. The drawknife, if used, requires the shaving-horse position or the work clamped securely in a vice. Oil-soaked rags with boiled linseed must be spread flat to dry before disposal.
The work
Step 1: Clean the shaft

If the bark has stayed on through drying, peel it with the sloyd knife: a series of short push cuts along the shaft at a low angle takes the bark cleanly. The hazel bark on a well-dried pole is thin and lifts off in long strips. Remove any knobs from side branches with the knife: cut flush to the shaft, travelling with the grain of the shaft.
Step 2: Shape the handle

The handle is the top 14 cm of the pole. Three handle shapes: a straight grip (the simplest, just a cleaned and chamfered shaft end), a thumb groove (a shallow carved recess on one side of the shaft for the thumb, 8 cm from the top, giving positive grip location), and a bulged knob handle (a wider carved oval 16 mm by 22 mm at the grip, narrowing below to the shaft diameter). For a thumb-groove handle, take push cuts from the centre of the groove toward each end to form a shallow hollow 6 mm deep by 30 mm long.
Step 3: Taper the tip

Cut the tip to the correct height (chin height when the stick is held at the maker's side). Take push cuts to form a gentle taper over the bottom 5 cm of the shaft, reducing to a rounded point. The tip can be left as a wood point or fitted with a metal ferrule; a rubber tip is the most practical for mixed indoor and outdoor use.
Step 4: Sand and finish

Sand the handle and any areas where the bark has been removed at 120 then 180 grit. Apply boiled linseed oil to the full length. Allow thirty minutes, wipe off. One coat on the shaft, two coats on the handle. Boiled linseed oil sets within 24 hours; the finished stick will shed light rain.
Variations

A thumb stick has a natural fork at the handle end, produced by cutting just below a side-branch fork. The Y-form cradles the thumb between the two tines. The fork must be sound and without rot; test by pressing hard with both thumbs before relying on it.
Ash produces a denser, heavier stick more suited to rough terrain. Cherry gives a finer-grained surface that polishes to a near-gloss under oil. Both require the same cutting approach.
Care
Re-oil once a year with boiled linseed. Store upright or horizontal to prevent permanent set. Do not leave submerged in water.

attrs.intro: Problems in pole selection and stick-making.

items.fix: A slight bow in the shaft is often correctable: warm the bent section with gentle heat from a heat gun (not direct flame) and press straight for ten minutes. A severe bow cannot be corrected without risk of splitting.
items.cause: The pole was dried on its side. The section that rested on the support bent under its own weight. The growth rings may also be uneven.
items.symptom: The dried pole has a pronounced bow in the middle

items.fix: Remove all remaining bark with the sloyd knife and sand the surface uniformly. A stick with a mix of barked and clean sections looks less finished than one with all-over bark or all-over bare wood.
items.cause: The pole was damaged at the cut site or had tight bark that dried and split rather than loosening cleanly.
items.symptom: The bark comes away unevenly during drying, leaving bare patches

attrs.heading: Common problems
